requirements: The core contributions of the paper 'AsyncTLS: Efficient Generative LLM Inference with Asynchronous Two-level Sparse Attention' have been reproduced.

sub_tasks.requirements: Required models and datasets are available for evaluation.
Code has been implemented to load Qwen3-8B for inference.

verification.spec: Run a Python script that loads Qwen/Qwen3-8B via HuggingFace transformers (AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer), runs a short generate() call on a sample prompt, and prints the decoded output. Verify the script exits with return code 0 and produces non-empty text output.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to load Qwen3-14B for inference.

verification.spec: Run the provided load script to instantiate Qwen3-14B via HuggingFace transformers and generate a 16-token completion from a short prompt. Assert generation completes without error and output is non-empty.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented to load GLM-4.7-Flash (MLA architecture) for inference.

verification.spec: Run a Python script that loads an MLA-architecture model (zai-org/GLM-4.5-Air as a stand-in for GLM-4.7-Flash) via HuggingFace transformers, performs a short generate() call on a sample prompt, and confirms the model exposes MLA-style compressed latent KV projections (e.g., q_a_proj/kv_a_proj_with_mqa or equivalent). Verify the script exits with return code 0 and outputs non-empty generated text.

sub_tasks.requirements: The LongBench benchmark (14 tasks: NarrativeQA, Qasper, MultiFieldQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiQA, Musique, GovReport, QMSum, MultiNews, TREC, TriviaQA, SAMSum, LCC, RepoBench-P, and PassageRetrieval) has been prepared for evaluation.

verification.spec: Run the data loader for LongBench and assert that the loaded dataset contains all 15 task names: NarrativeQA, Qasper, MultiFieldQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiQA, Musique, GovReport, QMSum, MultiNews, TREC, TriviaQA, SAMSum, LCC, RepoBench-P, PassageRetrieval. Assert each task has >=10 examples.

sub_tasks.requirements: The RULER benchmark (10 tasks: niah-single-1/2, niah-multikey-1/2, niah-multiquery, niah-multivalue, QA-Hotpot, QA-SQuAD, VT, FWE) has been prepared for evaluation.

verification.spec: Run the data loader for RULER and assert the 10 task subsets (niah-single-1, niah-single-2, niah-multikey-1, niah-multikey-2, niah-multiquery, niah-multivalue, qa-hotpot, qa-squad, vt, fwe) are available at context length 32k with non-empty splits.

sub_tasks.requirements: A calibration dataset D_cal has been prepared for use in selecting the top-d_c representative channels for token-level indexing.

verification.spec: Verify a calibration dataset path/loader exists in the repo and returns at least 32 calibration samples of token sequences usable for channel-importance computation (e.g., from C4, Pile, or WikiText).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Two-Level Sparse Attention (TLS) method described in Section 4.1 has been implemented.
Coarse-grained block-level selection has been implemented following the Quest-style scoring reformulated as GEMM operations.
Code has been implemented to partition the KV cache into m = floor(n/B) blocks of size B (with B=64 used in experiments).

verification.spec: Call the KV partitioning function with seq_len=1000 and B=64. Assert it returns m=floor(1000/64)=15 blocks each of size 64, plus optional handling of the residual 40 tokens. Repeat with seq_len=512, B=64 and assert m=8 with no residual.

sub_tasks.requirements: For each KV block i, the compressed block representations k_hat^max_i and k_hat^min_i (per-channel max and min of keys within the block) are computed and stored as the block-level index.

verification.spec: Build a random K of shape (n=256, d=64), partition into blocks of size B=64 (4 blocks), and call the block-index builder. Assert returned k_hat_max and k_hat_min have shape (4, 64) and equal the per-channel max/min of K within each block (compare elementwise to torch.amax/amin over block dim).

sub_tasks.requirements: For each block, the block importance score for a key-value group is computed as the sum over query heads sharing the same key of max(q^max . k_hat^max_i, q^min . k_hat^min_i)-style elementwise max-of-products, as defined in the paper's Quest reformulation.

verification.spec: Construct small q (G=4 heads, d=32), per-block k_hat_max/min (m=8 blocks, d=32). Compute block score via the method's implementation and compare to a reference implementation: for each block i, score_i = sum_{h in group} elementwise_max(q^max_h * k_hat_max_i, q^min_h * k_hat_min_i) summed over channels. Assert max abs diff < 1e-4.

sub_tasks.requirements: The block-level scoring has been reformulated and implemented as a standard GEMM between aggregated max/min query matrices Q^max/min (in R^{d_k x G}) and aggregated max/min key matrices K^max/min (in R^{d_k x N_b}) so it maps to dense matrix multiplication primitives.

verification.spec: Run the GEMM-reformulated scoring path and assert it produces results equal (within 1e-4) to the per-block scalar reference loop. Inspect the implementation to confirm it issues torch.matmul / cublas GEMM calls on shapes (d_k, G) x (d_k, N_b) (or transposed equivalents), not a python loop over blocks. Profiling: time GEMM path vs loop on N_b=512 and assert GEMM is at least 5x faster.

sub_tasks.requirements: The top-k_b blocks M_t with the highest block scores are selected per key-value group at each decoding timestep t.

verification.spec: Given fake block scores of shape (n_kv_groups=2, n_blocks=32) and k_b=8, call the top-k block selection. Assert output M_t has shape (n_kv_groups, 8) and that each row contains the indices of the 8 largest scores for that group (verify against torch.topk).

sub_tasks.requirements: The block selection retains 128 blocks per query (equivalent to 8,192 tokens given block size 64) as specified in the experiment setup.

verification.spec: Load the default AsyncTLS config and assert k_b == 128 and block_size B == 64, so that k_b * B == 8192 tokens are retained per query at the block level.

sub_tasks.requirements: Fine-grained token-level selection following Double Sparsity has been implemented within the blocks selected by the coarse stage.

Offline channel calibration on D_cal has been implemented, computing for each channel i an importance score by summing over heads the max absolute value of q_h(i) * k(i) and selecting the top-d_c channels as representative set C.

verification.spec: Run the offline channel calibration routine on a tiny synthetic model (8 heads, d=64) with D_cal of 16 samples. For each channel i in {0..d-1}, verify the produced score equals sum_h max(|q_h(i) * k(i)|) over the calibration tokens (compare to a reference numpy implementation, max abs diff < 1e-4). Verify the returned channel set C has exactly d_c elements and corresponds to the top-d_c scores.

sub_tasks.requirements: The token-level index dimension d_c is set to 32 for GQA models (Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-14B) and 128 for MLA architecture (GLM-4.7-Flash).

verification.spec: Load the config/launch scripts for Qwen3-8B, Qwen3-14B, and GLM-4.7-Flash. Assert the token-level index dimension d_c == 32 for the two Qwen3 (GQA) configs and d_c == 128 for the GLM-4.7-Flash (MLA) config.

sub_tasks.requirements: INT4 quantization is applied to compress the token-level key index footprint.

verification.spec: Pass a random K of shape (1024, 32) (float16) into the token-index quantizer. Assert the returned tensor uses 4 bits per value (e.g., packed uint8 with 2 values/byte yielding shape (1024, 16) bytes, or an int4 dtype). Dequantize and verify reconstruction error stays bounded (mean abs error < 0.1 * std(K)). Assert memory footprint is ~1/4 of the fp16 original (within 10%).

sub_tasks.requirements: Query and key vectors are projected onto the selected channels C to form compressed q_tilde and k_tilde used during token-level scoring.

verification.spec: Given q of shape (G, d=64), K of shape (n=256, d=64), and channel set C of size d_c=32, call the projection function. Assert q_tilde has shape (G, d_c) and equals q[:, C]; k_tilde has shape (n, d_c) and equals K[:, C] (after dequantization if quantized).

sub_tasks.requirements: Approximate per-token attention scores are computed by averaging softmax-like dot products of compressed query and key across the G query heads sharing the same key, as described in the method.

verification.spec: Run the approximate-score function with G=4 query heads sharing one KV head, compressed q_tilde (4, d_c) and k_tilde (n, d_c). Compare per-token output to reference: score_j = (1/G) * sum_h (q_tilde_h . k_tilde_j) / sqrt(d_c) (or method's exact formula). Assert max abs diff < 1e-4.

sub_tasks.requirements: The top-k_t tokens S_t are selected based on the approximate scores, and the selection is restricted to tokens that lie within the coarse-selected blocks M_{t-1}.

verification.spec: Construct M_{t-1} = {block indices [0,2,5]} with B=64, so candidate token positions = 192 specific positions. Run token-selector with k_t=32 on approx scores of length n=512. Assert all 32 returned token indices are within those 192 candidate positions (set membership check), and that they correspond to the 32 highest scores among those candidates.

sub_tasks.requirements: Final sparse attention output for each head h is computed using the full-dimensional KV pairs indexed by S_t with the standard scaled dot-product attention formula.

verification.spec: Given S_t (set of selected token indices) and full-dimensional K,V, call the sparse attention output function. Compare to a reference: out_h = softmax(q_h K[S_t]^T / sqrt(d)) V[S_t]. Assert max abs diff < 1e-4 on a random fp32 input.

sub_tasks.requirements: The token budget k_t for sparse attention is configurable to 512, 1024, and 2048 tokens to support the budget-sweep experiments.

verification.spec: Set the token_budget config option to 512, then 1024, then 2048, and for each verify that the AsyncTLS attention forward selects exactly that many tokens (len(S_t) == budget) on a synthetic input where n >> budget.

sub_tasks.requirements: The AsyncTLS asynchronous offloading engine and hierarchical KV cache management described in Section 4.2 have been implemented.
During prefilling, the block-level index and the token-level index are constructed over the input sequence's KV cache.

verification.spec: Run prefill on a synthetic 4k-token input through the AsyncTLS-wrapped model. After prefill, assert that for each layer the block-level index (k_hat_max, k_hat_min of shape (m, d_k)) and the token-level index (compressed/quantized K of shape (n, d_c)) are populated and non-empty.

sub_tasks.requirements: After prefilling, critical KV pairs (determined by the query representation of the final token) are retained in a GPU resident cache while remaining KV blocks are offloaded to CPU host memory.

verification.spec: After prefill on a 4k-token input, run the post-prefill offload step using the final-token query. Assert: (1) the GPU resident cache contains exactly k_b blocks selected by block-scoring with the final-token query; (2) the remaining (m - k_b) blocks' K,V tensors are stored in CPU pinned host memory (check tensor.device == 'cpu' and is_pinned()==True); (3) summed token count in resident cache equals k_b * B.

sub_tasks.requirements: A GPU resident cache C_t is maintained that stores the KV blocks corresponding to the most recently selected M set.

verification.spec: Instantiate the resident cache C_t and perform two simulated decoding steps with M_t differing between them. After each step, assert C_t.blocks contains exactly the block IDs in the most recent selection set, and the underlying K/V tensors for those blocks live on GPU (tensor.device.type == 'cuda').

sub_tasks.requirements: Incremental block transmission is implemented: at step t, only the set T_t = M_t \ M_{t-1} (blocks newly selected and not already resident) is transferred over PCIe, reducing bandwidth from O(k_b*B*d) to O(|T_t|*B*d) per step.

verification.spec: Construct a unit test that drives the offloading engine with two scripted block-selection sets M_{t-1} and M_t whose set-difference T_t = M_t \ M_{t-1} has a known size (e.g., |M_t|=128, overlap=120, |T_t|=8). Intercept the host->device transfer call (e.g., via a mock or counter on cudaMemcpyAsync / torch.Tensor.copy_) and assert that exactly |T_t| blocks of size B*d are copied, not |M_t|*B*d. Repeat across several (M_{t-1}, M_t) pairs and verify the transferred byte count equals |T_t|*B*d*dtype_size each time.

sub_tasks.requirements: The resident cache R-Cache is updated each step based on the incremental transfer set T_t.

verification.spec: Run the decoder for 5 consecutive steps with a mocked CPU KV store. After each step t, assert R-cache contents == (R-cache_{t-1} \ evicted) \union T_t where T_t = M_t \ R-cache_{t-1}. Verify by snapshotting R-cache block IDs before and after each step and comparing to the computed incremental set.

sub_tasks.requirements: Baseline attention methods used in the experiments have been implemented for comparison.
A Full Attention (FA) baseline that performs standard dense attention over the full KV cache has been implemented.

verification.spec: Import the FA baseline and run on a small input (batch=1, seq_len=128, d=64). Compare its output to torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention on the same Q,K,V; assert max abs diff < 1e-4. Confirm it attends over all keys (no masking beyond causal).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Quest block-level sparse attention baseline has been implemented with block size 64 and 128 selected blocks per query.

verification.spec: Inspect Quest baseline config / instantiate it: verify block_size == 64 and top_k_blocks == 128 (equivalent to 8192-token budget). Run one decoding step on a synthetic 32k context and check that exactly 128 blocks are selected per query/group and that the attention computation only attends to k_b*B = 8192 keys. Also check that block scoring uses Quest's max/min compressed block reps (per Eq. in Section 4.1).

sub_tasks.requirements: The Double Sparsity (DS) token-level sparse attention baseline has been implemented using offline channel calibration and top-k_t token selection across the full sequence (no block-level pre-filter).

verification.spec: Import the DS baseline module and instantiate it with a synthetic KV cache (seq_len=2048, d_head=64, n_heads=8). Confirm: (1) calibration step takes a small held-out tensor and produces a channel index set C of size d_c; (2) at decode, it computes approximate scores using only channels in C over the FULL sequence (not restricted to any block subset) and returns top-k_t indices; (3) output attention uses full-dim K,V at those indices. Verify no block-level pre-filtering occurs by checking that all n token positions are considered as candidates.
verification.paper_cite: Section 5, Experiment Setup; Yang et al. 2024

sub_tasks.requirements: GPU kernel implementations of all four attention variants are provided using TileLang for kernel benchmarking.
A TileLang kernel for Full Attention (FA) supporting GQA (32 heads, group size 4) and MLA (32 heads) has been implemented.

verification.spec: Import the TileLang FA kernel module and invoke it with two configurations: (a) GQA with num_q_heads=32, num_kv_heads=8 (group size 4), head_dim=128; (b) MLA with 32 query heads and the compressed latent KV layout. For each, run with random Q, K, V tensors at a context length of 32k, batch=1, and verify the kernel returns a tensor of the correct output shape without errors. Compare numerical outputs against a PyTorch reference scaled_dot_product_attention; assert max abs error < 1e-2.

sub_tasks.requirements: A TileLang kernel for block-level sparse attention (Quest) has been implemented.

verification.spec: Import the Quest TileLang kernel module. Provide synthetic Q (1 x 32 x 128), K/V (32k x 8 x 128 for GQA), block_size=64, top_kb=128. Verify the kernel runs without error, returns output of correct shape, and that for top_kb = num_blocks the output matches dense attention within atol=1e-2.

sub_tasks.requirements: A TileLang kernel for token-level sparse attention (DS) has been implemented.

verification.spec: Import the Double-Sparsity TileLang kernel. Configure with channel-compressed query/key (dc=32 for GQA, dc=128 for MLA), INT4-quantized key index, token budget k_t=512. Run on random inputs at seq_len=32k and assert kernel completes, returns correct shape, and recovers full-attention output within atol=5e-2 when k_t = seq_len.

sub_tasks.requirements: A TileLang kernel for the two-level sparse attention (AsyncTLS) has been implemented.

verification.spec: Import the TileLang AsyncTLS two-level sparse attention kernel and invoke it on a synthetic input: 32 query heads (GQA group size 4 and MLA configurations), context length 32k, batch=1, block size B=64, k_b=128 blocks selected, k_t=512 tokens. Verify the kernel executes without error and returns output of shape (batch, num_heads, head_dim). Sanity-check correctness by comparing against a slow Python reference that explicitly performs (i) block scoring with max/min compressed keys, (ii) top-k_b block selection, (iii) channel-compressed top-k_t token selection, (iv) full-dim attention over selected tokens; assert max abs error < 5e-2.

sub_tasks.requirements: Evaluation infrastructure for accuracy benchmarks has been implemented.
An evaluation harness for LongBench has been implemented that scores all 14 tasks reported in Table 1.

verification.spec: Run the LongBench harness with --tasks all-14 on a tiny stub model (e.g., a 2-layer randomly initialized transformer) and assert that scores are produced for exactly: NQA, QQA, MFQ, HQA, 2WM, Mus, GvR, QMS, MNs, TQA, PRetr, LCC, RBP and one TRec/SAMSum task (14 total per Table 1). Assert outputs are valid floats in [0,100].

sub_tasks.requirements: An evaluation harness for RULER has been implemented (using lm-eval-harness defaults consistent with the paper) that scores the 10 RULER subtasks.

verification.spec: Invoke `lm-eval --tasks ruler_niah_single_1,ruler_niah_single_2,ruler_niah_multikey_1,ruler_niah_multikey_2,ruler_niah_multiquery,ruler_niah_multivalue,ruler_qa_hotpot,ruler_qa_squad,ruler_vt,ruler_fwe` with the project's lm-eval-harness wrapper on a stub model. Assert all 10 subtasks return numeric scores and that the harness uses default lm-eval-harness configuration matching the paper.

sub_tasks.requirements: For GLM-4.7-Flash RULER evaluation, the S1, S2, MK1, and MK2 subtasks are excluded as noted in the paper.

verification.spec: Inspect the GLM-4.7-Flash RULER evaluation config/script. Assert that the task list explicitly excludes S1, S2, MK1, MK2 and includes only MQ, MV, QA-1, QA-2, VT, FWE (matching Table 3 columns).

sub_tasks.requirements: RULER is evaluated at a 32k context length with a sparse-attention token budget of 512 tokens.

verification.spec: Parse the RULER evaluation config and assert max_seq_len/context_length == 32768 and the AsyncTLS/Quest/DS token_budget (k_t or equivalent) == 512.

sub_tasks.requirements: LongBench is evaluated for Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B at 32k context length and for GLM-4.7-Flash at 128k context length, with the sparse-attention token budget set to 1024 by default.

verification.spec: Parse the LongBench config files. Assert: Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-14B configs set context_length=32768; GLM-4.7-Flash config sets context_length=131072 (128k); all three set sparse-attention token_budget=1024 for Table 1 runs.

sub_tasks.requirements: Accuracy experiments from the paper have been executed and reported numerical claims have been verified.
LongBench Qwen3-8B results at 32k context (token budget 1024) have been reproduced for Full, Quest, DS, and AsyncTLS.
Full Attention on Qwen3-8B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 50.14 (paper_category=claim, paper_value=50.14, tolerance ~1.0).

verification.spec: Run Full Attention inference on Qwen3-8B over the 14 LongBench tasks listed in Table 1 (NQA, QQA, MFQ, HQA, 2WM, Mus, GvR, QMS, MNs, TQA, PRetr, LCC, RBP and the 14th task per LongBench protocol) at 32k context, compute the per-task scores using the standard LongBench scorers, then take the macro-average across tasks. Verify the mean is ~50.14.

sub_tasks.requirements: Quest on Qwen3-8B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 47.49.

verification.spec: Run Quest (block-level sparse attention, block size 64, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-8B over the 14 LongBench tasks listed in Table 1 at 32k context, compute macro-average across tasks. Verify the average is ~47.49.

sub_tasks.requirements: Double Sparsity (DS) on Qwen3-8B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 50.10.

verification.spec: Run Double-Sparsity (token-level sparse attention, 32 selected channels, INT4 key-index quantization, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-8B across the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Verify the macro-average is ~50.10.

sub_tasks.requirements: AsyncTLS on Qwen3-8B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 49.90.

verification.spec: Run AsyncTLS (two-level: block size 64, 128 blocks selected at block-level; token-level channels=32, INT4, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-8B across the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Verify macro-average is ~49.90.

sub_tasks.requirements: LongBench Qwen3-14B results at 32k context (token budget 1024) have been reproduced for Full, Quest, DS, and AsyncTLS.
Full Attention on Qwen3-14B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 52.16.

verification.spec: Run Full Attention inference on Qwen3-14B across the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Compute macro-average. Verify result is ~52.16.

sub_tasks.requirements: Quest on Qwen3-14B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 49.33.

verification.spec: Run Quest sparse attention (block size 64, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-14B over the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Verify macro-average is ~49.33.

sub_tasks.requirements: DS on Qwen3-14B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 51.10.

verification.spec: Run Double-Sparsity (channels=32, INT4, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-14B across the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Verify macro-average is ~51.10.

sub_tasks.requirements: AsyncTLS on Qwen3-14B achieves a LongBench average of approximately 51.80 (best among baselines).

verification.spec: Run AsyncTLS (block size 64, 128 blocks; channels=32, INT4, token budget 1024) on Qwen3-14B across the 14 LongBench tasks at 32k context. Verify macro-average is ~51.80 and is highest among Full/Quest/DS/AsyncTLS for this model.

sub_tasks.requirements: LongBench GLM-4.7-Flash results at 128k context (token budget 1024) have been reproduced for Full, Quest, DS, and AsyncTLS.
Full Attention on GLM-4.7-Flash achieves a LongBench average of approximately 50.83.

verification.spec: Run Full Attention on GLM-4.7-Flash across the 14 LongBench tasks at 128k context. Compute macro-average. Verify result is ~50.83.

sub_tasks.requirements: Quest on GLM-4.7-Flash achieves a LongBench average of approximately 46.75.

verification.spec: Run Quest (block size 64, token budget 1024) on GLM-4.7-Flash across the 14 LongBench tasks at 128k context. Verify macro-average is ~46.75.

sub_tasks.requirements: DS on GLM-4.7-Flash achieves a LongBench average of approximately 50.73.

verification.spec: Run Double-Sparsity (channels=128 for MLA, INT4, token budget 1024) on GLM-4.7-Flash across the 14 LongBench tasks at 128k context. Verify macro-average is ~50.73.

sub_tasks.requirements: AsyncTLS on GLM-4.7-Flash achieves a LongBench average of approximately 50.58.

verification.spec: Run AsyncTLS (block size 64, 128 blocks; channels=128 for MLA, INT4, token budget 1024) on GLM-4.7-Flash across the 14 LongBench tasks at 128k context. Verify macro-average is ~50.58.

sub_tasks.requirements: Efficiency experiments and reported speedup/throughput claims from Section 5 have been reproduced.
Kernel-level speedups of AsyncTLS over Full Attention are reproduced.
For GQA, the AsyncTLS attention kernel delivers a 1.7x-6.2x speedup over Full Attention across batch sizes 1-8 and context lengths 32k-128k.

verification.spec: Run the GQA kernel-level benchmark across batch sizes {1,2,4,8} and context lengths {32k,64k,96k,128k} for both FA and AsyncTLS kernels. For each (bs, seq) cell, compute speedup = latency_FA / latency_AsyncTLS. Report the minimum and maximum speedup across the grid and compare to the paper's claimed range.

sub_tasks.requirements: For GQA, AsyncTLS delivers a 1.2x-4.0x speedup over the DS token-level kernel.

verification.spec: Run the GQA kernel-level benchmark across batch sizes {1,2,4,8} and context lengths {32k,64k,96k,128k} for DS and AsyncTLS kernels. Compute speedup = latency_DS / latency_AsyncTLS per cell. Report median speedup across the grid; assert it falls within the paper's claimed 1.2x-4.0x range.

sub_tasks.requirements: For MLA, AsyncTLS delivers a 3.3x-10.0x speedup over Full Attention.

verification.spec: Run the TileLang kernel-level benchmark for MLA (32 heads) comparing AsyncTLS vs Full Attention across batch sizes 1-8 and context lengths 32K-128K. Compute the ratio of FA latency to AsyncTLS latency for each configuration. Report the minimum and maximum speedup ratios; verify they fall in the claimed 3.3x-10.0x range.

sub_tasks.requirements: For MLA, AsyncTLS delivers a 1.9x-4.0x speedup over the DS token-level kernel.

verification.spec: Run the TileLang kernel-level benchmark for MLA (32 heads) comparing AsyncTLS vs token-level DS kernel across batch sizes 1-8 and context lengths 32K-128K. Compute ratios of DS latency to AsyncTLS latency. Verify the min/max speedups fall in the claimed 1.9x-4.0x range.

sub_tasks.requirements: AsyncTLS achieves approximately 54% of Quest's inference speed for GQA and 68% for MLA at the kernel level.

verification.spec: Run the TileLang kernel-level benchmark comparing AsyncTLS vs Quest for both GQA and MLA across batch sizes 1-8 and context lengths 32K-128K. For each architecture, compute the geometric mean (or representative average) of Quest_latency / AsyncTLS_latency. Verify GQA ratio ~= 0.54 and MLA ratio ~= 0.68.

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end latency results (without offloading) are reproduced.
On Qwen3-8B (without offloading), AsyncTLS achieves approximately a 2.3x end-to-end latency improvement over Full Attention.

verification.spec: Without KV cache offloading, measure per-token decoding latency of Qwen3-8B with Full Attention and AsyncTLS at the longest sequence length supported (e.g., 96k or 128k). Verify FA_latency / AsyncTLS_latency ≈ 2.3.

sub_tasks.requirements: On GLM-4.7-Flash (without offloading), AsyncTLS achieves approximately a 2.7x end-to-end latency improvement over Full Attention.

verification.spec: Without KV cache offloading, measure per-token decoding latency of GLM-4.7-Flash with Full Attention and AsyncTLS at the longest sequence supported (e.g., 96k). Verify FA_latency / AsyncTLS_latency ≈ 2.7.

sub_tasks.requirements: AsyncTLS end-to-end latency is faster than DS and approaches that of Quest on both Qwen3-8B and GLM-4.7-Flash.

verification.spec: Run end-to-end decoding latency benchmarks on Qwen3-8B and GLM-4.7-Flash across 32K-96K sequence lengths (no cache offloading) for FA, Quest, DS, and AsyncTLS. Assert that for each (model, seqlen) point: AsyncTLS_latency < DS_latency, and AsyncTLS_latency is within 1.3x of Quest_latency (i.e., AsyncTLS approaches Quest).

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end throughput results (with offloading) are reproduced.
At a 96k sequence length with offloading, AsyncTLS achieves approximately a 1.84x throughput improvement over Full Attention on Qwen3-8B.

verification.spec: Measure end-to-end decoding throughput (tokens/sec) of Qwen3-8B at 96k context length with KV cache offloading enabled. Compute Full Attention throughput at batch_size=1 (max it can fit) and AsyncTLS throughput at batch_size=6 (per paper). Verify AsyncTLS_tps / FA_tps is approximately 1.84.

sub_tasks.requirements: At a 96k sequence length with offloading, AsyncTLS achieves approximately a 4.70x throughput improvement over Full Attention on GLM-4.7-Flash.

verification.spec: Measure end-to-end decoding throughput of GLM-4.7-Flash at 96k context with KV-cache offloading enabled. Compare Full Attention (batch_size=1) vs AsyncTLS (batch_size=6). Verify AsyncTLS_tps / FA_tps is approximately 4.70.

sub_tasks.requirements: With KV cache offloading, AsyncTLS supports batch size 6 while Full Attention is constrained to batch size 1 in the throughput benchmark.

verification.spec: Run the throughput benchmark with KV cache offloading enabled at 96K context on a single H100. Verify that AsyncTLS successfully completes decoding with batch_size=6 (no OOM), and that Full Attention OOMs at batch_size>=2 so only batch_size=1 is feasible. Log peak GPU memory and assert AsyncTLS bs=6 fits while FA bs=2 does not.

sub_tasks.requirements: Environment, infrastructure, and analysis components are in place.
A GPU + CPU host setup with PCIe-connected pinned host memory for KV offloading is configured and used for the offloading throughput benchmark.

verification.spec: Verify the offloading benchmark setup: (1) pinned host memory is allocated for KV storage (check torch.cuda.is_pinned() on host buffers); (2) PCIe transfer streams are configured (non-default CUDA stream for D2H/H2D); (3) running the offloading entry point reports both GPU resident cache size and CPU offloaded cache size > 0 at 96K context.

sub_tasks.requirements: TileLang is installed and integrated into the build pipeline to compile the four attention kernels.

verification.spec: Run `python -c 'import tilelang; print(tilelang.__version__)'` to verify TileLang is installed. Then import the four attention kernel modules (AsyncTLS, DS, Quest, FA) from the repo and verify each exposes a compiled TileLang kernel callable (e.g., has a .build() or compiled JIT function attribute).

sub_tasks.requirements: lm-eval-harness is integrated and used with default configuration (with documented exceptions for GLM RULER subtasks).

verification.spec: Verify lm-eval-harness is installed (`python -c 'import lm_eval'`). Run a smoke test invoking the project's evaluation entry point on one RULER subtask (e.g., niah-single-1) with Qwen3-8B and confirm it loads the default lm-eval config. Verify documentation/code notes that S1/S2/MK1/MK2 are excluded for GLM-4.7-Flash.

sub_tasks.requirements: Plotting / analysis code that reproduces Figures 3 (budget sweep), 4 (kernel latencies), 5 (end-to-end latency), and 6 (throughput) from logged measurements is implemented.

verification.spec: Locate plotting scripts that produce Figures 3, 4, 5, 6 from logged CSV/JSON measurement files. Run each plotting script against a saved measurement log and verify it produces a PNG/PDF for each figure with the expected axes: Fig 3 (token budget vs accuracy per task), Fig 4 (batch_size/seqlen vs kernel latency for 4 methods), Fig 5 (seqlen vs end-to-end latency), Fig 6 (seqlen vs throughput).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the channel importance score (Section 4.1, picture [226 x 33]) aggregates max absolute values |q_h[i] * k[i]| across all G query heads sharing the key for each channel i, used in offline calibration on a held-out dataset.

verification.spec: Construct calibration inputs Q (G,d) and K (d) with known values; call channel_score(Q,K); verify per-channel output equals sum_h |q_h[i] * k[i]| within 1e-5; then verify top-d_c selection returns the correct channel indices.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the compressed key vectors used for token-level indexing are quantized to INT4 along the selected channels (Section 4.1, picture [197 x 15]), reducing index memory footprint.

verification.spec: Call compress_key(K, channels=C, bits=4); verify output dtype is int8/uint8 packed at 4 bits, only |C| channels retained, and dequantized values reconstruct K[:, C] within quantization error < 0.1 relative max.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the approximate token-level attention score (Section 4.1, picture [138 x 34]) averages compressed-channel dot products across the G query heads sharing the same key.

verification.spec: Given compressed q_tilde shape (G, |C|) and compressed k_tilde shape (N, |C|), verify approx_score(q_tilde, k_tilde) returns (1/G) * sum_h q_tilde_h . k_tilde for each token, matching reference within 1e-4.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that fine-grained token selection picks the top-k_t tokens by approximate score (Section 4.1, picture [140 x 13]) restricted to the candidate set produced by block-level selection M_{t-1}.

verification.spec: On synthetic scores over candidate token indices from M_{t-1}, call topk_token_select(scores, M_prev, k_t); verify returned set S_t has size k_t, is a subset of union of blocks in M_prev, and contains the k_t largest scores.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the final sparse attention output per head (Section 4.1, picture [152 x 33]) uses softmax over full-dimensional K_{S_t}, V_{S_t} indexed by the token-level selection set S_t.

verification.spec: On small synthetic Q,K,V and a known S_t, call sparse_attention_output(q_h, K, V, S_t); verify output equals softmax(q_h K[S_t]^T / sqrt(d)) V[S_t] within 1e-4, using full-precision K,V (not compressed).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the incremental block transmission set T_t = M_t \ C_{t-1} (Section 4.2, picture [128 x 12]) is computed correctly and only those blocks are transferred from CPU to GPU each step.

verification.spec: Simulate two consecutive block selections M_{t-1}, M_t with overlap; call incremental_transfer(M_t, resident_cache_C_{t-1}); assert returned T_t equals set difference M_t - C_{t-1}, and resident cache after update equals C_{t-1} union T_t intersected with M_t.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code has been implemented and run such that the staggered pipeline (Section 4.2, picture [136 x 28]) uses M_{t-1} for the current step's token-level selection S_t while concurrently computing M_t for the next step, creating a one-step lag.

verification.spec: Run a 4-step decoding simulation with the AsyncTLS scheduler; record at each step t which M_block_selection is used for token-level pruning; assert step t consumes M_{t-1} (not M_t), and that the block-selection-for-next-step kernel is launched on a separate CUDA stream concurrent with sparse attention.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the SnapKV observation-window baseline that identifies and compresses critical KV pairs prior to generation, integrated into the evaluation harness.

verification.spec: Import SnapKV baseline; run on a sample prompt; assert prefill-phase selects a configurable number of KV pairs based on an observation window, and that decoding produces output using only compressed cache.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the InfLLM block-level dynamic context-memory baseline for training-free long-context extrapolation.

verification.spec: Import InfLLM baseline; run decoding on a >32K context input; assert it uses block-level dynamic memory units and produces output within reasonable latency on a single GPU.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the Double-Sparsity (DoubleSparsity) baseline combining channel and token sparsity, as a distinct fully-featured baseline (not just the channel-selection subroutine used inside AsyncTLS).

verification.spec: Import DoubleSparsity baseline; run calibration to select important channels on a held-out set; perform decoding with channel+token sparsity at configurable budget; assert output is generated and channel set size matches configured d_c.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'post-training double-sparsity framework combining channel and token sparsity'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the PyramidKV baseline (pyramidal information-funneling KV compression, larger budgets at lower layers).

verification.spec: Import PyramidKV baseline; assert that the configured per-layer KV budget is monotonically non-increasing from lower to higher layers; run a short generation and confirm output is produced.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'PyramidKV introduces dynamic compression based on pyramidal information funneling'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the DynamicKV baseline with task-aware adaptive per-layer KV cache budget allocation.

verification.spec: Import DynamicKV baseline; verify it computes per-layer activation patterns and adapts KV budget allocation across layers based on task; run on two different task inputs and assert allocation differs.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'DynamicKV ... task-aware adaptive allocation'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the DuoAttention baseline distinguishing retrieval heads (full attention) from streaming heads (rolling cache).

verification.spec: Import DuoAttention baseline; verify head classification produces a binary mask over heads (retrieval vs streaming); run decoding and assert streaming heads use a fixed-size rolling cache while retrieval heads retain full KV.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'DuoAttention distinguishes between retrieval heads and streaming heads'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the Twilight hierarchical Top-p pruning baseline with fine-grained per-head sparsity gating.

verification.spec: Import Twilight baseline; verify it applies Top-p (nucleus) pruning over attention scores per-head with adaptive thresholds; assert per-head sparsity ratios vary across heads on a real input.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'Twilight designs hierarchical Top-p pruning with fine-grained gating'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the Star Attention two-phase block-sparse baseline (blockwise-local attention followed by sequence-global attention with sharded context).

verification.spec: Import Star Attention baseline; run two-phase attention on a long input (phase 1: blockwise-local per shard; phase 2: sequence-global); assert outputs match expected shape and that disabling phase 2 degrades accuracy on a retrieval probe.
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'Star Attention introduces a two-phase block-sparse mechanism'

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements the MagicPIG baseline using Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) sampling to approximate attention computation.

verification.spec: Import MagicPIG baseline; verify that LSH hash tables are built over keys and that queries sample candidate keys via hash collisions; run a short decode and assert attention is computed only over sampled subset (size < full sequence).
verification.paper_cite: Section 2 Related Works: 'MagicPIG employs Locality-Sensitive Hashing (LSH) sampling'

sub_tasks.requirements: FlexGen offloading baseline is implemented and runnable as a comparison point for long-context KV-cache offloading inference, exposing an interface that generates tokens with a chosen sequence length and reports throughput.

verification.spec: Import the FlexGen baseline wrapper, run end-to-end generation on a small (e.g., 8k-context) sample with offloading enabled, assert (a) it produces non-empty output, (b) reported peak GPU memory is less than would be needed for full in-GPU KV cache, and (c) a throughput (tokens/s) number is returned.

sub_tasks.requirements: InfiniGen dynamic KV cache offloading baseline is implemented as a comparison point, supporting decoding with prefetched KV cache from host memory.

verification.spec: Instantiate the InfiniGen baseline on a small LLM with a 16k-token prompt; assert that (a) generation completes, (b) the KV cache is partially resident on CPU as evidenced by GPU memory usage below the full-KV upper bound, and (c) reported decode latency per token is returned.

sub_tasks.requirements: ShadowKV KV offloading baseline is implemented as a comparison point, performing sparse attention with CPU-offloaded KV cache.

verification.spec: Run ShadowKV on a 32k-token prompt with a small model; assert (a) the wrapper exposes a generate() returning tokens, (b) accuracy on a 10-sample needle-in-a-haystack micro-task is >= 80%, and (c) per-step decode latency is logged.

sub_tasks.requirements: RetroInfer vector-storage offloading baseline is implemented as a comparison point for long-context inference.

verification.spec: Instantiate RetroInfer on a small LLM with 16k-32k context; assert (a) generation completes and returns plausible output, (b) a vector-index data structure (e.g., ANN index) is built over the KV cache, and (c) per-step latency is reported.

sub_tasks.requirements: Ablation studies from the paper

Code reproduces the token-budget ablation on Qwen3-14B with AsyncTLS, showing that increasing the token budget from 512 -> 1024 -> 2048 yields monotonic (non-decreasing) average LongBench-subset accuracy and that 2048-budget accuracy is within tolerance of full attention.

verification.spec: Run AsyncTLS on Qwen3-14B for the LongBench subset shown in Figure 3, sweeping token budgets {512, 1024, 2048}; compute average score across the subset for each budget; assert acc(2048) >= acc(1024) - 1.0 and acc(1024) >= acc(512) - 1.0, and assert acc(2048) is within tolerance of the reported Full Attention average (~52.16).

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements both incremental block transmission (transferring only T_t = M_t \ C_t) and a full-block-transfer variant; the ablation shows that incremental transmission moves substantially fewer bytes per decode step than full transfer, while producing identical attention outputs.

verification.spec: Simulate decoding for >=100 steps on a 32k context with block size 64 and kb=128. Measure mean blocks transferred per step under incremental mode (|T_t|) vs full mode (k_b). Assert mean(|T_t|) / k_b <= 0.5 (significant locality) and that attention outputs from both modes are bitwise (or within fp tolerance 1e-4) identical.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: fraction of k_b blocks transferred per step

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements both the GEMM-reformulated block scoring (Eq. for s_i via matmul on aggregated Q^{max/min}, K^{max/min}) and the original Quest per-block scoring; the ablation shows the GEMM form yields numerically equivalent scores while running faster on MLA/MQA.

verification.spec: On a synthetic input (n=32768, d=128, num_blocks=512, G=32), compute block scores with both implementations; assert max abs diff <= 1e-3 across all blocks. Then benchmark both on an MLA-style shared-KV setting and assert GEMM version is at least 1.5x faster than the original Quest scoring.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x speedup of GEMM vs original Quest scoring (MLA)

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements INT4 quantization of the token-level key index and compares it against an fp16 token-level index; the ablation shows INT4 reduces index memory by ~4x while incurring negligible accuracy loss on a small in-context retrieval task.

verification.spec: On Qwen3-8B with token budget 1024, run RULER niah-single-1 (or similar) with token-level index stored as (a) fp16 and (b) INT4; assert accuracy_int4 >= accuracy_fp16 - 2.0 points and assert measured GPU index memory footprint with INT4 is <= 0.3 * fp16 footprint.

sub_tasks.requirements: Code implements both staggered (asynchronous) prefetch where token-level selection at step t uses block selection M_{t-1}, and a synchronous variant where M_t is computed and prefetched before attention at step t; the ablation shows the async variant has lower per-step latency with negligible accuracy degradation.

verification.spec: On Qwen3-8B with 32k context and KV offloading to CPU, decode 256 tokens under both modes; assert async per-step latency is at least 1.3x lower than sync, and that text output overlap (BLEU or exact-match prefix of >=200 tokens) is >= 0.9 between the two modes on a deterministic (greedy) prompt.

sub_tasks.paper_unit: x speedup of async vs sync prefetch

sub_tasks.requirements: Hardware / library platforms used in the paper
All four attention-kernel variants (AsyncTLS, DS, Quest, FA) used in the operator-level benchmark are implemented in TileLang, and importing them confirms TileLang is the kernel-authoring framework.

verification.spec: importlib.import_module('tilelang') succeeds; the four kernel modules each contain a tilelang.jit-decorated function or equivalent TileLang program object; running one kernel on a tiny input produces a tensor of the expected shape.

sub_tasks.requirements: RULER evaluation is run through lm-evaluation-harness (lm-eval-harness), as in the paper's evaluation pipeline.

verification.spec: importlib.import_module('lm_eval') succeeds; the RULER task names used by the paper (niah-single-1, niah-multikey-1, ruler_qa_hotpot, etc.) are registered in lm_eval's task registry; a smoke-test run of one RULER subtask on a tiny model completes and returns a numerical score.

sub_tasks.requirements: End-to-end throughput experiments with KV-cache offloading are run on a system with a PCIe-connected CPU-GPU interconnect, and the harness reports the measured PCIe bandwidth used during offloaded decoding.

verification.spec: Query the GPU's host interconnect (e.g., via nvidia-smi topo -m or torch.cuda.get_device_properties) and assert the bus is PCIe (not solely NVLink to CPU). During an offloaded AsyncTLS decode run, measure CPU->GPU transfer throughput and assert it is within tolerance of a realistic PCIe bandwidth (e.g., 16-64 GB/s for PCIe Gen4/Gen5 x16).
